_meta.description: Live grounded catalog — seed capabilities + pipeline-verified products.

capabilities.name: Flagship model
capabilities.description: Each provider's most capable general-purpose model.

capabilities.name: Consumer chat assistant
capabilities.description: Primary conversational app for end users across web, desktop, and mobile.

capabilities.name: Agentic coding
capabilities.description: Autonomous or assisted software development across terminal, IDE, and cloud surfaces.

capabilities.name: Remote / async agent control
capabilities.description: Starting, monitoring, and directing an agent (esp. a coding agent) from a remote device or asynchronously.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic surfaces async control through interface-native handoffs (Claude Code on the web, Move tasks between web and terminal, Claude Code in Slack), OpenAI through infrastructure-native execution (Remote connections, Cloud environments, Codex Security cloud), and Google through orchestration-native delegation (Asynchronous Subagents, Multi-Agent Teamwork).

capabilities.name: Browser / computer-use agent
capabilities.description: An agent that drives a browser or computer on the user's behalf to complete multi-step web tasks.

capabilities.name: Knowledge-work / research agent
capabilities.description: Non-developer agent for documents, analysis, and research grounded in user sources.

capabilities.name: Agent-building SDK / framework
capabilities.description: Developer toolkit for building, orchestrating, and deploying custom agents.

capabilities.name: Enterprise agent platform / deployment
capabilities.description: Managed platform for building, deploying, governing, and running agents and models at enterprise scale.

capabilities.name: Image / video generation
capabilities.description: First-party models or products that generate images or video.

capabilities.name: Managed agent runtime
capabilities.description: Provider-hosted execution of agents: the provider runs the agent loop and sandboxes its tools, instead of you self-hosting.
capabilities.comparison_note: For managed agent runtime, Anthropic is sandbox-native, isolating execution via Sandbox runtime alongside Routines and Claude Code on the web; OpenAI is policy-native, governing runtime through Automations and its Permissions and security model; Google is project-native, structuring runtime around Projects, Sidecars, Subagents, and Hooks.

capabilities.name: MCP & connectors
capabilities.description: Model Context Protocol support: hosted MCP servers, connector registries, and managed tool connectors.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers MCP on managed configuration (managed-mcp.json) and installation scopes, OpenAI treats MCP as transport-and-server-native (server transports, Codex as an MCP server), while Google leans store-native distribution via an MCP Store and mcp permission.

capabilities.name: Agent memory
capabilities.description: Persistent, cross-session memory an agent can read and write across runs.
capabilities.comparison_note: For agent memory, Anthropic is file-native (CLAUDE.md, auto-managed MEMORY.md, Output styles), OpenAI relies on a single file-native AGENTS.md, while Google takes a config-native approach through structured Rules and Workflows.

capabilities.name: Code execution & sandbox
capabilities.description: Server-side code execution in an isolated sandbox for agent actions.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic offers container-native isolation through Dev containers and Docker-backed Containers, OpenAI takes a policy-native approach with Sandbox modes, Permission profiles, and Approval policy, while Google centers on terminal-native control via Terminal Sandboxing and a Fine-Grained Permissions Engine.

capabilities.name: Agent evals & observability
capabilities.description: Tracing, evaluation, and monitoring of agent runs.
capabilities.comparison_note: For agent evals and observability, Anthropic is telemetry-native via OpenTelemetry monitoring, cost tracking, and debug logging; OpenAI is platform-native with Tracing, Trace grading, an Evals API, and Datasets and eval runs; Google is runtime-native through Observability, Subagents, and Lifecycle Hooks.

capabilities.name: Guardrails & safety
capabilities.description: Policy and safety controls on an agent's inputs, outputs, and actions.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers safety on a config-driven Permission system with Permission modes and Managed settings, OpenAI on runtime isolation via Sandbox mode plus an Approval policy and Permission profiles, while Google takes a policy-native approach through its Fine-Grained Permissions Engine, Allowlist/Denylist, and Strict Mode.

capabilities.name: Subagents & orchestration
capabilities.description: Spawning and coordinating multiple agents / subagents within a single task.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers definition-native subagents with Custom subagents, Forked subagent, and Worktree isolation; OpenAI takes a workflow-native path through Subagent workflows and Orchestration; Google is lifecycle-native, foregrounding Subagent Lifecycle and States and Inter-Agent Communication.

capabilities.name: Unclassified (intake)
capabilities.description: Holding bucket for newly discovered offerings/features not yet mapped to a capability. The discovery pipeline lands items here as candidates; triage assigns the real capability.

No comparable autonomous deep research feature found.

products.scope_note: Deploy and manage Claude models on external third-party platforms.

products.scope_note: Google's agentic-coding platform (Antigravity umbrella): the Antigravity IDE/desktop app (2.0 preview), the Antigravity CLI, and the Jules async web agent.

products.scope_note: Anthropic ships no first-party image or video generation model; the Claude family is text- and code-only.

lifecycle.note: Modeled absence — operator-verified.

products.name: Google managed agents
products.scope_note: # Agents overview Stay organized with collections Save and categorize content based on your preferences. Build, deploy, and manage AI agents that use reasoning and tools to perform complex enterprise tasks, such as automating workflows, discovering information across multiple sys

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 0.67). Google's managed agents offering lets enterprises build, deploy, and manage reasoning/tool-using agents that Google hosts and runs — the core of a managed agent runtime (provider runs the agent loop). It is broader th

products.name: Subagents
products.scope_note: Specialized AI assistants that run in their own isolated context window, handle a delegated task, and return a summarized result to the main conversation.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Subagents are specialized assistants that run in isolated context windows, handle delegated tasks, and return summarized results to the main conversation — a direct match for spawning and coordinating multiple subagen

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Subagents are specialized AI assistants that run in isolated context windows, handle a delegated task with tailored system prompts and restricted toolsets, then return a result to the main conversation. Google and OpenAI both offer comparable constructs, though with differing emphasis on async parallelism and orchestration depth.

equivalents.note: Also delegates tasks to parallel subagents with isolated execution, but emphasizes async multi-threading and tracked state over context/tool isolation.
Also spawns specialized agents in parallel and consolidates results, but lacks explicit per-agent context isolation or tailored system prompt framing.

products.name: Built-in Subagents
products.scope_note: Pre-configured subagents automatically invoked by the system when a task warrants delegation.

products.name: Explore Subagent
products.scope_note: Fast, read-only built-in subagent for searching and analyzing codebases.

products.name: Plan Subagent
products.scope_note: Research-focused built-in subagent that gathers context during plan mode.

products.name: General-Purpose Subagent
products.scope_note: Built-in subagent designed to handle complex, multi-step tasks.

products.name: Context Isolation
products.scope_note: Each subagent runs in its own context window; only a summary is returned to the parent.

products.name: Per-Agent Tool Restrictions
products.scope_note: Limit which tools a subagent can use via a dedicated frontmatter configuration field.

products.name: Tailored System Prompts
products.scope_note: Give subagents specialized instructions and domain knowledge through custom system prompts.

products.name: Skill Preloading
products.scope_note: Preload reusable skill definitions into a subagent via a dedicated skills configuration field.

products.name: Custom Subagents
products.scope_note: Users can create their own subagents with custom prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is a feature of Claude Code's subagents capability allowing users to define custom subagents with their own prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills. It directly fills the subagents-orchest

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Custom Subagents let users define subagents with custom prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills via filesystem, CLI, or programmatic definitions. Google's nearest equivalent supports similar dynamic custom subagent creation with custom system prompts and tools; OpenAI's is more limited, using TOML files for model config and instructions without the same depth of permission/hook control.

equivalents.note: Also supports dynamic custom subagents with custom prompts and tools, but no explicit filesystem/CLI/programmatic definition modes.
TOML-based config for model and instructions only; lacks permission modes, hooks, and skill definitions.

products.name: Subagent Scope Types
products.scope_note: Define subagents at managed, session, project, user, or plugin scope levels.

products.name: Scope Precedence & Override
products.scope_note: Higher-priority scopes override same-named subagents in lower-priority scopes.

products.name: Filesystem-Based Agent Definition
products.scope_note: Define subagents as markdown files stored in the .claude/agents/ directory.

products.name: Programmatic Agent Definition
products.scope_note: Define subagents at runtime via the agents parameter in the query() API.

products.name: CLI Agent Definition
products.scope_note: Pass subagent definitions as JSON at Claude Code launch for the session.

products.name: Dynamic Agent Configuration
products.scope_note: Generate agent configuration dynamically at query time based on runtime conditions.

products.name: Persistent Agent Memory
products.scope_note: Subagents accumulate and retain insights across conversations via a memory directory.

products.name: Background Agent Execution
products.scope_note: Run a subagent as a non-blocking background task alongside other operations.

products.name: Forked Subagent
products.scope_note: A subagent that continues from / inherits the full current conversation context instead of starting fresh.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). A forked subagent is a specific subagent-spawning mechanism that inherits the full current conversation context rather than starting fresh. This is a narrow feature/slice within Claude Code's subagents & orchestration

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Forked Subagent spawns a subagent that inherits the full current conversation context rather than starting with a blank slate, enabling continuity-aware delegation. Neither Google nor OpenAI offers a direct equivalent; their subagent features always initialize with fresh or separately defined contexts.

equivalents.note: Delegates tasks to parallel subagents but does not fork/inherit parent conversation context.
Spawns specialized subagents in parallel but does not carry over the parent conversation context.

products.name: Conversation Fork
products.scope_note: Spawns a subagent by branching the existing conversation context at the current point.

products.name: Agent Teams
products.scope_note: Coordinate multiple independent Claude Code sessions working together as a team, led by a team lead, with a shared task list and peer-to-peer messaging.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Agent teams coordinate multiple independent Claude Code sessions working together with a team lead, shared task list, and peer-to-peer messaging — this is squarely multi-agent spawning and coordination, i.e. subagents

comparison.summary: Agent Teams coordinates multiple Claude Code sessions as a named team with a designated lead, shared task list, peer-to-peer messaging, and lifecycle hooks. Google's nearest equivalent is more framework-oriented with async coordination; OpenAI's is thinner, relying on MCP plumbing rather than a first-class team abstraction.

equivalents.note: Similar collaborative multi-agent goal, but no explicit team-lead role or shared task list UI.
Achieves multi-agent coordination via MCP/SDK plumbing; lacks native team structure, task list, or peer messaging.

products.name: Shared Task List
products.scope_note: Distributed work queue where agents claim, track, and complete tasks with states and dependencies.

products.name: Agent Peer-to-Peer Messaging
products.scope_note: Mailbox system enabling direct message passing between individual agents.

products.name: Agent Display Modes
products.scope_note: Render agent sessions inline in terminal or in split panes via tmux or iTerm2.

products.name: Plan Approval Gate
products.scope_note: Hold agents in read-only planning mode until the team lead explicitly approves their plan.

products.name: Scoped Subagent Definitions
products.scope_note: Assign teammates using subagent definitions sourced from any configured scope.

products.name: Agent Lifecycle Hooks
products.scope_note: Trigger custom quality-gate logic on agent idle, task creation, or task completion events.

products.name: Agent Dispatch & Monitor View
products.scope_note: Unified CLI view to dispatch and monitor all background agent sessions.

products.name: Dynamic Workflows
products.scope_note: A JavaScript script Claude writes that orchestrates subagents at scale and runs in the background, cross-checking their results for work too big to coordinate one turn at a time.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Dynamic workflows is a Claude-authored JavaScript orchestration script that spawns and coordinates subagents at scale, runs in the background, and cross-checks their results — a direct subagents-orchestration capabili

comparison.summary: Dynamic Workflows lets Claude write a JavaScript orchestration script that coordinates parallel subagents at scale in the background, cross-checking their outputs across multiple turns. Google's Multi-Agent Orchestration and OpenAI's Multi-Agent Workflows cover similar ground but neither wraps the full pipeline—natural-language authoring, plan approval, live progress, mid-session resume—into a single saved, reusable workflow primitive.

equivalents.note: Covers orchestration with error recovery but lacks NL authoring, plan approval, and mid-session resume.
MCP-based multi-agent setup; no integrated NL authoring, progress view, or mid-session resume.

products.name: Natural-Language Workflow Authoring
products.scope_note: Trigger Claude to generate a workflow script via keyword or natural-language prompt.

products.name: Ultracode Mode
products.scope_note: Named mode applying maximum reasoning effort with automatic workflow orchestration.

products.name: Workflow Slash Command Saving
products.scope_note: Persist a generated workflow as a reusable slash command for future invocations.

products.name: Pre-Run Plan Approval
products.scope_note: Per-run prompt displaying planned phases for user approval before execution begins.

products.name: Live Run Progress View
products.scope_note: Real-time dashboard showing workflow phases, agent counts, token usage, and elapsed time.

products.name: Mid-Session Workflow Resume
products.scope_note: Resume a paused workflow in the same session, reusing cached results from completed agents.

products.name: Concurrency & Scale Limits
products.scope_note: Supports up to 16 concurrent agents and 1,000 total agents per workflow run.

products.name: Bundled Orchestration Skills
products.scope_note: Pre-built prompt playbooks enabling Claude to orchestrate tasks and spawn agents automatically.

products.name: Worktree Isolation
products.scope_note: An isolation mode that runs agents in separate git worktrees / checkouts so parallel agents don't overwrite each other's files.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Worktree isolation is a mechanism enabling multiple parallel agents to operate without overwriting each other's files by giving each its own git worktree/checkout. This is a supporting slice of subagents/orchestration

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Worktree Isolation runs each agent in its own separate git worktree/checkout, preventing parallel agents from colliding on shared files. Google's Projects and OpenAI's Sandbox Modes offer the closest analogues, but neither specifically uses git worktrees — they isolate by folder/repository boundaries or filesystem permission scopes instead.

equivalents.note: Isolates agents by folder/repo boundaries and scoped settings, but not via per-agent git worktrees.
Defines filesystem boundaries for agents, but uses OS-level sandboxing, not git worktree isolation.

products.name: Per-Session Git Checkout
products.scope_note: Creates a dedicated git checkout per session to prevent file conflicts across parallel agents.

products.name: Subagent Workflows
products.scope_note: Codex spawns specialized agents in parallel and collects their results in one consolidated response.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is Codex's capability to spawn specialized agents in parallel and consolidate their results — a direct subagents/orchestration feature. It is a sub-feature of the existing Codex subagents-orchestration product. I

comparison.summary: OpenAI Codex's Subagent Workflows spawns specialized agents in parallel and consolidates their results into one response. Anthropic's nearest equivalent uses JavaScript-orchestrated subagents at scale, while Google's Asynchronous Subagents similarly delegates parallel background tasks to concurrent agents; both cover the core parallel-dispatch-and-collect pattern.

equivalents.note: JS-scripted orchestration of subagents at scale; adds background execution and cross-checking not explicit in Codex's feature.
Parallel async subagent delegation without blocking; lacks explicit CSV batch processing trigger model.

products.name: Explicit Subagent Triggering
products.scope_note: Subagent workflows only launch when explicitly requested, never spawned automatically.

products.name: CSV Batch Subagent Processing
products.scope_note: Spawns one subagent per CSV row and exports their combined results.

products.name: Cross-Agent Orchestration
products.scope_note: Codex handles cross-agent orchestration including spawning, routing instructions, waiting for results, and closing threads.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This offering is Codex's cross-agent orchestration mechanism — spawning subagents, routing instructions, waiting for results, and closing threads — which is squarely the subagents-orchestration capability. It is a sub

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Cross-Agent Orchestration lets Codex spawn subagents, route instructions, await results, and close threads — covering the full lifecycle of multi-agent coordination. Anthropic's nearest match is Agent Teams, and Google's is Multi-Agent Orchestration; both handle multi-agent coordination but differ in architecture and automation depth.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude sessions via a team lead, but uses a shared task list and peer-to-peer model rather than explicit spawn/route/close thread lifecycle.
Covers orchestration with error recovery and retries, but is a broader framework rather than a single agent (Codex) managing explicit thread lifecycles.

products.name: Subagent Sandbox & Approvals
products.scope_note: Subagents inherit sandbox policy and surface approval requests from inactive threads to the orchestrator.

products.name: Subagent Management
products.scope_note: You can switch between active agent threads and steer, stop, or close running subagents.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This offering covers steering, stopping, closing, and switching between running subagent threads — a control/management slice of the broader subagents-orchestration capability rather than the full spawn-and-coordinate

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Subagent Management lets users actively switch between running agent threads and steer, pause, or terminate individual subagents mid-execution. Anthropic's nearest equivalent is more passive (no direct thread-switching UI), while Google's Subagent Lifecycle & States covers state control but lacks explicit thread-switching.

equivalents.note: Handles delegation and isolation but lacks explicit thread-switching or live steer/stop controls.
Covers running/idle/killed state management but does not expose thread-switching between active agents.

products.name: Agent Thread Switching
products.scope_note: Inspect and switch between concurrently running agent CLI threads.

products.name: Custom Agents
products.scope_note: You can define your own custom agents with different model configurations and instructions via TOML files.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Defining custom agents with distinct model configurations and instructions via TOML files is a subagents/orchestration feature of OpenAI Codex, directly enabling multiple agent definitions. It maps to Codex's subagent

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Custom Agents lets users define reusable agent configurations — including model, instructions, reasoning effort, and tool access — via TOML files. Both Anthropic and Google offer analogous custom subagent definition capabilities, though neither uses TOML files and both are more tightly integrated into their respective agentic frameworks rather than standalone config files.

equivalents.note: Defined via prompts/permissions/hooks rather than TOML; no per-agent model or reasoning effort selection.
Defined dynamically with custom prompts and tools, but via code/runtime rather than static TOML config files.

products.name: Built-in Agent Presets
products.scope_note: Pre-configured default, worker, and explorer agents available out of the box.

products.name: Per-Agent Model Selection
products.scope_note: Assign specific models to each agent, either auto-selected or pinned.

products.name: Per-Agent Reasoning Effort
products.scope_note: Set high, medium, or low reasoning effort independently for each agent.

products.name: Subagent Role & Tool Config
products.scope_note: Assign specialized roles and toolsets to individual subagents.

products.name: Multi-Agent Workflows
products.scope_note: By exposing Codex CLI as an MCP server and orchestrating it with the Agents SDK, you can build multi-agent workflows.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This describes a pattern for building multi-agent workflows by exposing Codex CLI as an MCP server and orchestrating it via the Agents SDK — directly about spawning and coordinating multiple agents within a task, henc

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Multi-Agent Workflows uses Codex CLI exposed as an MCP server, orchestrated via the Agents SDK, to build pipelines where multiple agents hand off tasks and progress is visible in a tracing dashboard. Anthropic offers a comparable Agent Teams feature, and Google provides Multi-Agent Teamwork, both enabling coordinated multi-agent task execution but through different orchestration primitives and tooling.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude Code sessions as a team with a lead agent, but no MCP-server-mode CLI or SDK-based orchestration.
User defines a high-level goal and platform manages agent coordination, but lacks an MCP-server-exposed CLI and explicit hand-off tooling.

products.name: Codex MCP Server Mode
products.scope_note: Run Codex CLI as an MCP server so other agents can invoke it as a tool.

products.name: codex Tool (Session Start)
products.scope_note: MCP tool that starts a new Codex session from an initial prompt.

products.name: codex-reply Tool (Session Continue)
products.scope_note: MCP tool that continues an existing Codex session using a thread ID.

products.name: Single-Agent Workflow
products.scope_note: Coordinate two agents together in a minimal single-agent workflow pattern.

products.name: Project Manager Multi-Agent Workflow
products.scope_note: A Project Manager agent coordinates task hand-offs across a team of specialized agents.

products.name: Agent Hand-offs
products.scope_note: Transfer control and context between agents within a multi-agent workflow.

products.name: Workflow Tracing Dashboard
products.scope_note: Records and displays prompts, tool calls, and hand-offs in a Traces dashboard.

products.name: Subagent Context Isolation
products.scope_note: Each subagent runs in its own isolated context, separate from the parent agent.

products.name: Parallel Subagent Fan-Out
products.scope_note: Distribute work across multiple subagents running simultaneously in parallel.

products.name: Subagent Lifecycle & States
products.scope_note: Subagents run asynchronously in one of three states: Running, Idle, or Killed.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This describes the lifecycle/state model (Running, Idle, Killed) of subagents running asynchronously — a specific aspect of subagent orchestration rather than the full orchestration capability, hence 'partial'. It is

comparison.summary: Google's Subagent Lifecycle & States defines three explicit runtime states (Running, Idle, Killed) for asynchronously executing subagents, with Auto-Wake resuming idle agents automatically. Anthropic has no direct lifecycle state model; OpenAI's Subagent Management is the closest match, letting users switch between, steer, stop, or close active agent threads.

equivalents.note: No direct equivalent; no explicit lifecycle state model for subagents.
Allows steering/stopping threads, but no formal Running/Idle/Killed state model or auto-wake.

products.name: Auto-Wake
products.scope_note: Automatically wakes an idle subagent when new work is available.

products.name: Inter-Agent Communication
products.scope_note: Agents communicate by sending messages to each other using unique agent IDs, with flexible routing and shared transcripts.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Inter-agent communication (message passing between agents via unique IDs, flexible routing, shared transcripts) is a mechanism for coordinating multiple agents — a slice of subagents/orchestration rather than the full

comparison.summary: Google's Inter-Agent Communication lets agents address and message each other directly by unique agent IDs, with flexible routing and shared transcripts. Neither Anthropic nor OpenAI offer an exact equivalent; both provide orchestration/coordination primitives but not explicit agent-to-agent messaging via IDs with shared transcripts.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude sessions as a team, but uses a lead/peer model rather than direct ID-based messaging with shared transcripts.
